one_line_takeaway: The first systematic benchmark of SLM jailbreak resistance shows nearly half of 63 edge-oriented small models are highly jailbreakable, that training choices (SFT vs DPO, distillation, architecture) matter far more than parameter count, and that existing prompt-level defenses fail against semantic attacks.
key_finding: SLMs are broadly and unreliably vulnerable: 47.6% of the 63 models are highly susceptible (average ASR > 0.4) and 38.1% cannot resist even a plain DirectRequest (ASR > 0.5). GCG is the strongest attack (avg ASR > 50% across most models). Counterintuitively, robustness depends more on training details than model size: SFT models are 10-40% more robust than their DPO counterparts (DPO exhibits 'compliance drift' — initially refusing, then complying), knowledge distillation degrades safety (DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B jumps to 0.271 ASR on DirectRequest vs 0.086 for Qwen2.5-1.5B), and larger/more-capable models can be MORE vulnerable to semantic attacks. Architecture matters: RecurrentGemma-2B (Griffin recurrent architecture) is the single most robust model (avg ASR 0.14). No prompt-level defense is adequate — PPL filtering crushes suffix attacks like GCG (0.52 to ~0.03) but does nothing against semantic attacks like DirectRequest (0.39 to 0.39); Self-Reminder gives at most a 10% ASR reduction and helps only models that already have security awareness.
core_contribution: The first large-scale empirical study of small language models' (SLMs) susceptibility to jailbreak attacks. The authors build SJBench, a fully automated evaluation framework, and run it over 63 instruction-tuned SLMs from 15 mainstream families (100M-7B parameters) against 8 jailbreak methods (5 white-box: GCG, AutoPrompt, PEZ, GBDA, UAT; 3 black-box: DirectRequest, HumanJailbreaks, PAP), measuring harmfulness (ASR), output diversity, and fluency. They answer three research questions: how vulnerable SLMs are (RQ1), which factors drive vulnerability (RQ2), and how well three prompt-level defenses work (RQ3). The paper's own contribution is the SLM-focused benchmark and the factor analysis; the jailbreak methods, the 70-question class-balanced dataset (from Xu et al.), and the defense methods are all taken from prior work and applied here, not invented by this paper.
threat_model: An adversary aims to craft prompts that bypass an SLM's safety alignment and elicit harmful content across 14 risk categories (e.g., Illegal Activity, Malware, Physical Harm, Fraud). Both white-box adversaries (access to gradients/architecture, used for GCG/AutoPrompt/PEZ/GBDA/UAT with 20-token suffixes optimized over 500 steps) and black-box adversaries (API/output-only access, used for DirectRequest/HumanJailbreaks/PAP) are considered. The paper explicitly excludes prompt injection, sponge/resource-exhaustion, and privacy attacks (data extraction, membership inference). The authors argue SLMs raise the threat because edge deployment (smartphones, IoT, smart vehicles) lowers the attack barrier, makes attackers hard to trace via local deployment, and prevents cloud-side content-moderation guardrails from blocking unsafe outputs.
limitations: Scope is English-only, decoder-only, instruction-tuned SLMs, excluding RWKV/Mamba variants and non-English safety. The 70-question dataset is small (5 per category), and ASR relies entirely on a single HarmBench Llama-2-13B judge whose errors propagate directly into every number. Several cells are missing (e.g., UAT not run for Gemma-2/RecurrentGemma/TinyLlama/OLMo/Dolly; HumanJailbreaks missing for MiniCPM-S-1B and Dolly-v1), so per-model averages aren't over a uniform attack set. Only three prompt-level defenses are tested — no model-level defenses (adversarial training, alignment retraining, output filtering) are evaluated, and the paper's PAP uses an in-context substitute because the official paraphraser is unavailable, and its Self-Reminder is placed in the input rather than system prompt, both of which weaken those methods versus their original forms. Factor-analysis claims (size, data, distillation) are largely observational correlations across heterogeneous families rather than controlled ablations.

datasets_used: Class-balanced jailbreak dataset from Xu et al.: 70 harmful questions spanning 14 risk categories (5 per category), derived from OpenAI/Meta usage policies

63 instruction-tuned SLMs across 15 families (LLaMA-3.2, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen, Qwen/1.5/2/2.5, Gemma family, Phi-3/3.5, MiniCPM, H2O-Danube, SmolLM, StableLM, TinyLlama, MobileLLaMA, MobiLlama, Fox, Dolly, OLMo), 100M-7B parameters
quantitative_results: 47.6% of the 63 SLMs are highly vulnerable (average ASR > 0.4); 38.1% have ASR > 0.5 on DirectRequest
GCG is the most effective attack, averaging over 50% ASR across most models
RecurrentGemma-2B-it is the most robust model at average ASR 0.14; reduces ASR by 35% vs Gemma-2B-it on PEZ and GBDA
Most vulnerable individual models include StableLM-2-1.6B-Chat (avg 0.675), SmolLM-1.7B-Instruct (avg 0.671), TinyLlama-1.1B-Chat-v1.0 (avg 0.658), H2O-Danube-1.8B-Chat (avg 0.605)

SFT vs DPO: SFT models are 10-40% more robust; MiniCPM-2B-SFT-bf16 has 27% lower ASR than MiniCPM-2B-DPO-bf16 on DirectRequest (0.186 vs 0.457); OLMo-7B-SFT-hf 23% lower than OLMo-7B-Instruct-hf on PEZ
Distillation harms safety: DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B DirectRequest ASR 0.271 vs 0.086 for Qwen2.5-1.5B; distilled 7B 0.514 vs 0.257 for base
SmolLM HumanJailbreaks ASR rises from 40.6% (135M) to 64.9% (360M/1.7B) with scale/instruction fidelity
PPL defense drops GCG average ASR from 0.52 to ~0.03 but leaves DirectRequest unchanged (0.39 to 0.39)
Retokenization reduces ASR below 0.3 for most models (e.g., GCG 0.52 to 0.20) but Group-II models stay 0.3-0.5
Self-Reminder yields at most 10% ASR reduction; strong on Group-I (32-74% of original value on GCG) but negligible or slightly worse on Group-II (MobileLLaMA ASR increases)
baselines_compared: DirectRequest (vanilla harmful query) used as the baseline to gauge raw robustness against complex attacks
White-box attacks: GCG, AutoPrompt, PEZ, GBDA, UAT
Defense baselines: Perplexity filtering, Retokenization (BPE-dropout), Self-Reminder
